node.title: Uber OA - US Mid / Entry Level

article.title: Uber OA - US Mid / Entry Level
article.content:
Here is one of the questions I was asked on my 2 question - 90 minute OA and the respective solution.

Please study undirected trees if you are going to take Uber OA and traversal throguh them with adjacency list. Please be aware of undirected vs directed trees. And be comfortable with DFS or BFS. Best of luck!

```
from collections import defaultdict, deque

def find_path_in_tree(edges, start, end):
    """
    Find path from start to end in an undirected tree.
    
    Args:
        edges: List of tuples representing edges [(u, v), ...]
        start: Starting node
        end: Ending node
    
    Returns:
        List representing the path from start to end
    """
    # Build adjacency list
    graph = defaultdict(list)
    for u, v in edges:
        graph[u].append(v)
        graph[v].append(u)
    
    # BFS to find path
    queue = deque([(start, [start])])
    visited = set([start])
    
    while queue:
        node, path = queue.popleft()
        
        if node == end:
            return path
        
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            if neighbor not in visited:
                visited.add(neighbor)
                queue.append((neighbor, path + [neighbor]))
    
    return []  # No path found

def find_path_dfs(edges, start, end):
    """
    Alternative DFS approach to find path.
    """
    graph = defaultdict(list)
    for u, v in edges:
        graph[u].append(v)
        graph[v].append(u)
    
    visited = set()
    def dfs(node, path):
        if node == end:
            return path
        
        if node in visited:
            return []
        
        visited.add(node)
        
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            # if neighbor not in visited:
            result = dfs(neighbor, path + [neighbor])
            if result:
                return result
        
        visited.remove(node)
        return None
    
    return dfs(start, [start])

def find_path_dfs2(edges, start, end):
    """
    Alternative DFS approach to find path.
    """
    graph = defaultdict(list)
    for u, v in edges:
        graph[u].append(v)
        graph[v].append(u)
    
    visited = set()
    def dfs(node, path):
        if node == end:
            return path
        
        if node in visited:
            return []
        
        visited.add(node)
        
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            # if neighbor not in visited:
            result = dfs(neighbor, path + [neighbor])
            if result:
                return result
        
        visited.remove(node)
        return None
    
    return dfs(start, [start])

# Example usage:
edges = [(1, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (4, 5), (4, 6)]
start = 1
end = 6

path = find_path_in_tree(edges, start, end)
print(f"Path from {start} to {end}: {path}")
path = find_path_dfs(edges, start, end)
print(f"Path from {start} to {end}: {path}")
path = find_path_dfs2(edges, start, end)
print(f"Path from {start} to {end}: {path}")
# Output: Path from 1 to 6: [1, 2, 4, 6]


```

post.content:
I can think of a better approach-

1) do one round of dfs - (as its undirected trree every node will have just one parent no cycles)
     - find node 
     - also strore parent of each tree.

2) now do this start from node ans traverse all the way to end using the parent pointers

i wouldn\'t be surprised if Uber tests heavily on graph based questions given that\'s the inherent nature of their businesses. In fact, Google tests heavily on this sort of questions too. Some hard DFS questions can be challenging to solve
